title: Hedging with Talent: How Managers Use Selection to Navigate Novel Technology
data_source: Company X (Pitney Bowes): 59,021 temp exit records, 2,506 permanent exits, automation project timelines, 69 interviews

categories.framing: Automation causes turnover among temp workers (H1-H5 hypothesis-testing)
categories.mechanism: Direct causal effect: automation → job threat → worker exit
categories.evidence: 59K temp exits, 21 automation projects across 12 facilities
categories.claims: H1: Automation increases turnover. H2: Sorters/robots substitute → higher turnover. H3: Anticipation effect (workers quit before automation). H4: Productivity-churn trade-off. H5: Exit composition shifts to resignations.
categories.architecture: Standard hypothesis-testing quant paper: Intro → Lit Review → Methods → Results → Discussion
categories.robustness: Planned: TWFE DiD, event study, Callaway-Sant'Anna, placebo tests

events.summary: Initial draft as causal quant paper
events.detail: 9 section drafts created in single day (Nov 21). Framed as hypothesis-testing paper targeting Management Science or JLE. Core claim: automation increases turnover among temp workers. No volume controls yet. Hypotheses H1-H5 structured around causal predictions.
events.trigger: Aniket Panjwani tweet about Claude Code for EDA; dormant data from 2020-2021 warehouse study

categories.validation: ZIP CRITIQUE: Packaged option_cultivation_FULL_PACKAGE_20251126.zip for external model review
categories.workflow_refinement: Zip critique process: package manuscript + data + code, send to external AI for rigorous adversarial review

events.summary: Zip critique: sent to external model
events.detail: Packaged full paper + data + code into option_cultivation_FULL_PACKAGE_20251126.zip and sent to powerful external AI model for rigorous adversarial review. Package included: manuscript.tex, data files, analysis scripts, REVIEW_PROMPT.md with specific verification requests for quantitative robustness and qualitative evidence checking.

categories.evidence: Core finding killed: 'automation increases turnover by 40-50%' collapses with volume control (p=0.63)
categories.claims: KILLED: Global turnover effect. KILLED: Sorter +112% effect. SURVIVED: Pre-treatment anticipation pattern (18-19pp drop).
categories.robustness: Volume control added → main effect disappears. Pre-treatment patterns survive.
categories.validation: External 'friendly review' delivers devastating feedback: 'expect a hard reject at ASQ/ManSci'

events.detail: Adversarial review (Dec 8-9) reveals: (1) automation-turnover effect is entirely confounded with volume—high-volume sites automate AND have more exits; (2) supervisor/manager heterogeneity is 'too unstable to treat as strong empirical pillar'; (3) theoretical positioning of 'option cultivation' too close to job crafting and standard real options. Verdict: 'hard reject in current form'. Only the pre-treatment anticipation pattern survives.
events.trigger: Zip critique returned from external AI reviewer

categories.framing: Workers don't flee approaching automation—they stay. The puzzle is why.
categories.mechanism: Multiple candidates: (1) Option value—stay to see outcomes, (2) Reduced hiring pipeline, (3) Selection—fearful workers already left, (4) Job lock, (5) Curiosity/attachment
categories.evidence: Pre-treatment drop in voluntary exits: Sorters -19.1pp (22.4%→3.3%), Robots -18.3pp (40%→21.7%)
categories.claims: NEW: Workers don't flee automation. NEW: Voluntary resignations decrease sharply before implementation.

events.detail: Immediate pivot after external review. Dropped the 'automation causes turnover' claim entirely. Reframed paper around the robust pre-treatment pattern: voluntary exits decrease as automation approaches. New title options: 'Staying to See', 'The Anticipation Paradox', 'Waiting for the Robots'. Mechanism now exploratory rather than confirmatory.
events.trigger: External review killed main effect; needed to build paper around what survived

events.version_name: HALLUCINATION DISCOVERED: Wait-and-See Evidence Never Existed

categories.mechanism: CRITICAL FAILURE: Sensemaking mechanism was built on evidence that doesn't exist in transcripts
categories.evidence: HALLUCINATION: Adversarial audit of 69 transcripts found NO worker quotes supporting 'wait and see' curiosity. Paper had assumed workers expressed curiosity about automation—they didn't.
categories.claims: INVALIDATED: 'Workers engage in anticipatory sensemaking' has no qualitative support
categories.validation: Adversarial audit searched for 'wait and see', 'curious', 'want to know' patterns across 69 transcripts. Found 12 matches—all researcher statements or tangential mentions. Zero first-person worker quotes expressing curiosity.
categories.automation_added: Living Paper system built: claim-evidence traceability for IRB-protected data
categories.workflow_refinement: /audit-claims step added to workflow before drafting

events.summary: HALLUCINATION: Wait-and-see qualitative evidence never existed
events.detail: Dec 13: Adversarial audit revealed the 'Wait and See' mechanism was built on fabricated/assumed evidence. Searched 69 transcripts for worker expressions of curiosity about automation. AUDIT FOUND: 'NO clear first-person worker quotes expressing curiosity about automation.' Additionally found: (1) 75% of workforce shows 'really no care' about automation (EV-008), (2) Manager explicitly states immigrants 'don't wait' (EV-007). The entire sensemaking mechanism was unsupported—AI had either fabricated quotes or assumed worker attitudes that don't appear in the data. This, combined with the seasonality confound, killed the 'Wait and See' framing.
events.trigger: Living Paper audit against raw transcripts

categories.validation: Comprehensive audit completed: claims linked to evidence across 69 interviews

events.detail: Dec 13-14: Built Living Paper infrastructure to handle verification of claims backed by IRB-protected qualitative data. System creates bidirectional links between claims and hashed evidence. Reviewers can see verification status without accessing raw transcripts. Created /audit-claims command.
events.trigger: Need to verify claims without exposing protected data to reviewers

categories.mechanism: Added: 'intuitive awareness' vs explicit knowledge. Workers respond to uncertainty holistically, not labeled information.
categories.evidence: NEW: Mosio SMS survey data added (156 workers, Oct 2020-Aug 2021). Job search declined during implementation (29.8%→24%). Only 6% of 'heard about changes' responses mentioned technology.
categories.claims: NEW: Managers' information embargo was 'leaky'—workers picked up signals through proxy channels despite management silence.
categories.boundaries: Added: 92% opt-out rate on survey (survivorship bias); single facility (ONT); COVID confound

events.detail: Integrated SMS survey data from 156 temp workers at Bloomington facility. Key finding: workers framed changes operationally (hours, pay) not technologically—only 6% mentioned automation explicitly. This supports 'wait and see' but via 'intuitive awareness' rather than explicit knowledge. Added 'leaky embargo' concept: managers tried to hide automation plans but information leaked through proxy signals.
events.trigger: Reviewer critique: 'Can't directly measure worker knowledge of upcoming automation'

events.version_name: Wait and See Debunked → Hedging with Talent

categories.framing: KILLED: 'Wait and See' (sensemaking mechanism unsupported). NEW: 'Hedging with Talent'—manager-side selection mechanism
categories.mechanism: KILLED: Workers making sense of uncertainty. NEW: Managers select on reliability + withhold job-threat info → reliable (low-turnover) workers stay longer
categories.evidence: Tenure diff-in-diff: Robot facilities +66h (22h→76h); Sorter facilities -12h. 95% CI [56h, 79h]. Mann-Whitney p<0.001
categories.claims: NEW: Tenure increases 4x at novel-tech (robot) facilities. NEW: No change at mature-tech (sorter) facilities. Selection mechanism, not worker sensemaking.
categories.boundaries: Added: N=1 robot facility limitation. Novel vs mature technology as key moderator.
categories.validation: Validation revealed sensemaking mechanism unsupported—workers couldn't 'make sense' if managers withheld information
categories.replication: Validation package created with full quantitative replication

events.detail: Jan 8: Debunked 'Wait and See' hypothesis after discovering seasonality confound (H1→H2 drop exists company-wide in 2019 with no automation). More critically: validation revealed workers couldn't be 'making sense' because managers deliberately withheld information. Pivoted to manager-side mechanism: under technological uncertainty, managers select temps on reliability and withhold job-threat info, creating a workforce 'hedge'. This explains why tenure increases at novel-tech (robot) facilities but not mature-tech (sorter) facilities.
events.trigger: Seasonality analysis debunked wait-and-see; validation revealed sensemaking mechanism unsupported

categories.mechanism: Formalized: Selection under technological uncertainty. Key citations: Sverke (2002), Greenhalgh & Rosenblatt (1984), Jovanovic (1979)
categories.boundaries: Boundary condition: permanent employees show opposite pattern (tenure decreases)—they have more information and agency
categories.automation_added: /eval-zuckerman-lite completed: Puzzle in the world? YES. Compelling null? YES.

events.detail: Ran theory-forge workflow: /explore-data, /hunt-patterns, /eval-zuckerman-lite, /find-theory, /find-lens. Identified primary theory violated (Job Insecurity/Displacement) and sensitizing literature (Org Learning Under Uncertainty + Labor Market Sorting). Key bridge: technological novelty → uncertainty → selection based on reliability → retention effect.
events.trigger: Needed theoretical grounding for new hedging mechanism

events.detail: Commit 164da3e: 'Initial commit: warehouse automation papers (manuscripts only)'. Project formally tracked in git.

categories.claims: Softened: 'tests confirm' → 'tests indicate'; 'data support the prediction' → 'data are consistent with the expectation'
categories.register: Theory-building language scrub: removed all hypothesis-testing framing

events.detail: Commit 78cb070: Systematic replacement of hypothesis-testing language with theory-building alternatives. 'validates' → 'indicates'; 'confirms' → 'suggests'; 'tests confirm' → 'tests indicate'. Rationale: theory-building papers discover patterns; they don't test hypotheses.
events.trigger: Recognition that inductive papers require different register

categories.mechanism: Named phenomena introduced: 'Reliability Hedging' (selection), 'Information Shielding' (withholding implications), 'The Compositional Shift' (resulting tenure patterns)
categories.architecture: MAJOR: Intro now puzzle-only (removed mechanism preview). Theory → 'Prior Work and the Displacement Puzzle' with 'What Prior Work Assumes' subsection—ends with research question, not answer. Findings restructured around named phenomena. Quant integrated last.
categories.automation_added: Qual-inductive rubric built: 10 criteria, 50 pts. Genre-specific validation rules for inductive papers.

events.summary: Major restructure to ASQ/OrgSci inductive genre
events.detail: Commit 528ee73: Complete restructuring to align with inductive theory-building conventions. Key moves: (1) Introduction presents puzzle only, not mechanism; (2) Theory section renamed and restructured—ends with question, not answer; (3) Findings = theory development via named phenomena; (4) Quantitative patterns integrated after qualitative discovery. Prior score ~24/50 on qual-inductive criteria → estimated ~40-45/50 post-restructure.
events.trigger: Generic paper rubrics failed qual papers; needed genre-specific structure

categories.length: MAJOR expansion: 6,895 → 11,037 words. Intro: 366→1,021. Theory: 1,169→2,512. Methods: 1,130→1,663. Findings: 2,573→3,246. Discussion: 1,494→2,429.

events.summary: Git: Expanded to meet ASQ/OrgSci word count
events.detail: Commit f102d69: Expanded paper from ~7K to ~11K words to meet ASQ/Organization Science requirements. Added deeper puzzle setup in intro, new theory subsection on technological uncertainty, richer methods setting description, 3 additional quotes on selection practices, extended discussion of contribution and boundary conditions.
events.trigger: Journal word count requirements (10K-12K for ASQ)

categories.evidence: 11 bibliography entries added: job insecurity foundations (Sverke, Greenhalgh), psychological contracts (Dekker, Rousseau, Zhao), temp work/embeddedness (Mitchell, Ashford, Feldman), org theory (Galbraith, Thompson), methodology (Locke)

events.detail: Commit 13f44c0: Added 11 missing bibliography entries to support expanded theory and methods sections.

events.version_name: Final Agent Review: Ready with Minor Fixes

categories.claims: Reviewer flags tautology residue in selection→retention link; N=1 limits theoretical reach
categories.boundaries: Need evidence that Skaro deployment was organizationally important, not just routine. N=1 limitation needs stronger tempering.
categories.validation: Three independent reviewers (ASQ Standards, Methods/Evidence, Writing/Framing). Verdict: Ready with minor fixes. No submission-blocking issues. Of 15 items from PRESUBMISSION_REVISION_LIST_v2: 8 fully resolved, 6 partially resolved, 1 not addressed.
categories.replication: Replication package and validation package created (Feb 6)

events.summary: Final pre-submission review: ready with minor fixes
events.detail: Feb 6, 2026: Three independent agent reviewers assessed the manuscript. Key findings: (1) Tautology weakened but not dissolved (selection on reliability → retained reliable workers), (2) N=1 novel-tech site limits theoretical reach, (3) Need stronger evidence Skaro was organizationally important, (4) Section 2.3 derivation risks appearing post hoc, (5) Need deeper engagement with sorting literatures (Jovanovic, Autor, Lazear). Verdict: addressable in 1-2 days of focused revision.
